notes.section_title: Introduction: Importance of Population in Social Studies

content.text: Population is the pivotal element in social studies. It is the point of reference from which all other elements are observed and derive significance and meaning. Resources, calamities, and disasters are all meaningful only in relation to human beings. People are important to develop the economy and society. The numbers, distribution, growth, and characteristics of population provide the basic background for understanding all aspects of the environment.

Human beings are producers and consumers of earth's resources. Therefore, it is important to know how many people are in a country, where they live, how and why their numbers are changing, and what their characteristics are.
An official enumeration of population conducted periodically to collect demographic, social, and economic data.

In India, the first census was conducted in 1872. The first complete census was taken in 1881. Since then, censuses have been held regularly every tenth year. The Indian Census is the most comprehensive source of demographic data.

India's population as of March 2011 stood at 1,210.6 million (approximately 1.21 billion), which accounts for more than 17 percent of the world's population. This large population is distributed over India's vast area of 3.28 million square kilometres, which accounts for only 2.4 percent of the world's area.
India has overtaken China in 2023 to become the most populous country in the world. This represents a significant demographic shift in global population distribution.

India's 1.21 billion people are unevenly distributed across the country. Uttar Pradesh with a population of 199 million is the most populous state, accounting for about 16 percent of the country's population. In contrast, Sikkim has only 0.6 million people and Lakshadweep has just 64,429 people.

Almost half of India's population lives in just five states: Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Bihar, West Bengal, and Andhra Pradesh. Despite being the largest state by area, Rajasthan has only 5.5 percent of India's total population, demonstrating the uneven nature of distribution.
The number of persons per unit area, typically expressed as persons per square kilometre.

India is one of the most densely populated countries in the world. Only Bangladesh and Japan have higher average population densities than India. India's average population density in 2011 was 382 persons per square kilometre, but densities vary dramatically from state to state.

Bihar has the highest population density at 1,102 persons per square kilometre, while Arunachal Pradesh has the lowest at only 17 persons per square kilometre. This variation reflects differences in terrain, climate, rainfall, and soil fertility across regions.

content.title: Factors Influencing Population Density
content.text: States with densities below 250 persons per square kilometre have rugged terrain and unfavourable climatic conditions. Assam and peninsular states have moderate densities due to hilly terrain and moderate rainfall. Northern plains and Kerala have high densities due to flat plains, fertile soils, and abundant rainfall.
The change in the number of inhabitants of a country or territory during a specific period of time.

Population growth can be expressed in two ways: absolute numbers (magnitude of increase) and percentage change per year (annual growth rate). Absolute increase is the difference between later and earlier population figures. Annual growth rate is expressed as percentage per annum, indicating the increase per 100 persons in the base population.

India's population has steadily increased from 361 million in 1951 to 1,210.6 million in 2011. From 1951 to 1981, the annual growth rate was steadily increasing, but since 1981, the growth rate has been declining gradually due to declining birth rates.

content.title: Paradox of Growth Rate Decline
content.text: Despite declining growth rates since 1981, the absolute number of people being added to the population continues to increase. This occurs because the growth rate is applied to an increasingly large base population. When a low rate is applied to over a billion people, the absolute increase remains substantial.

Population is dynamic, constantly changing due to three main processes: birth rates, death rates, and migration. The natural increase of population is the difference between birth rates and death rates.
The number of live births per thousand persons in a year. It is a major component of population growth because in India, birth rates have historically been higher than death rates.
The number of deaths per thousand persons in a year. The main cause of population growth rates in India has been the rapid decline in death rates through improved healthcare and sanitation.

Till 1980, high birth rates combined with declining death rates resulted in high population growth rates. Since 1981, birth rates have also started declining gradually, causing a corresponding decline in overall growth rates.

The movement of people from one place to another. Can be internal (within a country) or external (between countries). Internal migration does not change total population size but affects distribution. External migration changes both size and distribution.

Migration affects population in source areas (net outflow/loss) and destination areas (net inflow/gain). Internal migration has significantly influenced India's population distribution, with movement generally from rural to urban areas and from economically backward to developed regions.
The composition of population by age groups (young, working-age, and elderly). Shows proportion of dependent versus economically active population.
The ratio of people of dependent age (below 15 and above 60 years) to people of economically active ages (15-59 years).

India has a relatively young population with a significant proportion below 15 years of age. This creates both opportunities and challenges: the large youth population represents future workforce but requires substantial investment in education and healthcare. A high dependency ratio means fewer economically active people must support more dependents.
The number of females per thousand males in a population.

Sex ratio is an important indicator of population composition and social development. It reflects gender-based inequalities in society. India's sex ratio has been a concern, with some regions showing significant female deficit due to various socio-economic factors.
The percentage of people in a population who can read and write. According to census definition, a literate person is one who can read and write any language with understanding.

Literacy is crucial for socio-economic development. Regional variations in literacy reflect differences in development levels and education infrastructure. Higher literacy correlates with lower fertility rates and better health outcomes.

The National Population Policy (NPP) 2000 represents a comprehensive approach to population management and social development. It provides a policy framework addressing population issues through integrated measures.

Key objectives of NPP 2000 include: providing free and compulsory school education up to 14 years of age, reducing infant mortality rate to below 30 per 1,000 live births, achieving universal immunisation of children against vaccine-preventable diseases, promoting delayed marriage for girls, and making family welfare a people-centred programme.

content.title: Philosophy of NPP 2000
content.text: Rather than directly mandating population control, the NPP 2000 focuses on social development and empowerment, recognizing that improvements in education, health, and quality of life lead naturally to demographic transition and lower fertility rates.

India's annual increase in population is large enough to neutralise efforts to conserve the resource endowment and environment. Despite declining growth rates, the absolute additions remain substantial due to the large population base. Every year, India adds millions of people, creating enormous challenges for housing, employment, education, and resource management.

The declining trend of growth rates is a positive indicator of birth control efforts. However, sustaining this trend and managing the existing large population remains a significant development challenge for the nation.

glossary.definition: The total number of people living in a particular area or country at a given time.
An official enumeration of population conducted periodically to collect demographic, social, and economic data.
The total number of people in a region, typically expressed in millions or billions.
The spatial arrangement of population, showing how people are spread across a geographical area.
The number of people per unit area, usually expressed as persons per square kilometre.
The change in total population number over a period of time, expressed as absolute increase or percentage.
The actual number of people added to the population during a specific period.
The percentage increase in population per year, calculated as increase compared to base population.
The number of live births per thousand persons in a year.
The number of deaths per thousand persons in a year.
The difference between birth rates and death rates, representing population change from natural processes.
Movement of people from one place to another, can be internal or external.
Movement of people within a country from one region to another.
Movement of people between different countries (immigration and emigration).
Composition of population by age groups (young, working-age, elderly).
The ratio of dependent population (below 15 and above 60) to economically active population (15-59).
The number of females per thousand males in a population.
The percentage of population that can read and write with understanding.
Cities with a population of more than one million or 10 lakh people.
Government framework for addressing population issues through social development and welfare programmes.

summary_points: India's population of 1,210.6 million (March 2011) represents 17.5% of world's population on 2.4% of world's land area; unevenly distributed with half living in five states (Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Bihar, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh); population density varies from 1,102 persons/sq km (Bihar) to 17 persons/sq km (Arunachal Pradesh).

Population growth increased steadily from 361 million (1951) to 1,210.6 million (2011); growth rate increased till 1981 (2.22%) then declined to 1.64% (2011); paradox: despite declining rates, absolute additions increase due to large base population; India overtook China in 2023 to become most populous country.

Three processes drive population change: birth rates (historically higher in India), death rates (rapidly declined through improved health), and migration (internal: rural-to-urban movement; external: immigration-emigration).

Population characteristics include age structure (young population below 15), dependency ratio (dependent vs economically active), sex ratio (females per 1000 males), and literacy rates (vary significantly regionally).

National Population Policy 2000 aims for social development through free compulsory education (age 14), reducing infant mortality (below 30 per 1000), universal immunisation, promoting delayed marriage, and people-centred welfare programmes; focuses on development-driven demographic transition rather than direct population control.
